topic.what: Nobel Prize-winning discovery that aspirin and aspirin-like drugs inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX), thereby blocking prostaglandin synthesis
topic.how: In-vitro experiments demonstrating that aspirin, indomethacin, and sodium salicylate inhibit prostaglandin synthetase activity in cell-free preparations from guinea pig lung, correlating inhibitory potency with anti-inflammatory clinical efficacy
topic.why_matters: Established the fundamental mechanism of action for NSAIDs — COX inhibition and prostaglandin synthesis blockade — which explains both their therapeutic (anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antipyretic) and adverse (gastric mucosal injury, renal) effects

characteristics.what_it_measures: Identification of COX as the molecular target of aspirin and related drugs
characteristics.value: Aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase enzyme, blocking conversion of arachidonic acid to prostaglandins
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior to this work, the mechanism of aspirin's anti-inflammatory action was unknown despite decades of clinical use
characteristics.context: Cell-free enzyme preparations from guinea pig lung homogenates; subsequently confirmed across species and tissues

good_for: Understanding the molecular basis of NSAID-induced gastropathy
Explaining why prostaglandin depletion compromises gastric mucosal defense
Foundation for rational COX-2-selective inhibitor design
requires: Basic understanding of prostaglandin biochemistry and arachidonic acid metabolism

novelty: First identification of cyclooxygenase as the pharmacological target of aspirin — a drug used for over 70 years without known mechanism
Established the prostaglandin hypothesis of inflammation, enabling rational NSAID design
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (1982)
open_problems: COX-1 vs COX-2 isoform distinction was not yet known (discovered in the 1990s)
Tissue-specific prostaglandin roles and consequences of selective inhibition were unexplored
Non-COX mechanisms of aspirin (acetylation of other proteins) were not addressed
